title: Get started
text: test compares two independent binomial proportions \(u/m\) \(v/n\), testing \(H_0: \theta = \theta_0\) \(\theta\) log odds ratio. default odds_ratio = 1 (equality proportions). reproduce Example 2 paper’s Table 2: 13 41 versus 6 47. result htest, usual components available directly, match Table 2 (odds ratio 3.173, p-value 0.034, 95% CI 1.082 10.25):
code: library(modifiedfisher) result <- modified_fisher_exact_test(u = 13, m = 41, v = 6, n = 47,                                      odds_ratio = 1, local_size_data = TRUE) result #>  #>  Non-Conservative Size-α Modified Fisher's Exact Test #>  #> data:  u = 13, v = 6, m = 41, n = 47 #> p-value = 0.03369 #> alternative hypothesis: true odds ratio is not equal to 1 #> 95 percent confidence interval: #>   1.082336 10.249348 #> sample estimates: #> odds ratio  #>   3.172619 result$estimate #> odds ratio  #>   3.172619 result$p.value #> [1] 0.03369141 result$conf.int #> [1]  1.082336 10.249348 #> attr(,"conf.level") #> [1] 0.95

title: Get started
text: modified_fisher_exact_test() implements two-sided test . null hypothesis \(H_0\): = odds_ratio, alternative two-sided: \(\neq\) odds_ratio. title: Get started
text: power functions can inverted answer planning question: given target power, sample sizes needed? closed-form answer, simplest approach grid search candidate values \(m\) \(n\). example finds smallest equal-allocation sample size (\(m = n\)) needed achieve 80% power \(\pi_1 = 0.3\), \(\pi_2 = 0.6\) \(H_0\): = 1 \(\alpha = 0.05\): smallest \(n\) achieving 80% power 44 per group, giving 80.1% power. search stops first \(n\) crosses threshold; inspect results see power grows sample size. unequal allocation, replace loop grid pairs \((m, n)\). Note power calculation re-runs full test optimisation candidate sample size, search slow large candidate ranges. coarse first pass (step size 5 10) followed fine search around crossing point efficient.
code: target_power <- 80   # percent pi1 <- 0.3 pi2 <- 0.6 alpha <- 0.05   # Search over candidate n = m from 10 to 100 results <- data.frame(n = integer(), power = numeric())   for (n_candidate in 10:100) {      df_candidate <- construct_test_frame(     .odds_ratio = 1, .m = n_candidate, .n = n_candidate,     .alpha = alpha, .precision = 1e-3   )      g0_candidate <- optimise_gamma0(     .odds_ratio = 1, .m = n_candidate, .n = n_candidate,     .alpha = alpha, .precision = 1e-3,     .method = "zoom", .maze = 10, .zoom_iter = 6   )      pwr <- power_modified(     p          = c(pi1, pi2),     .gamma0    = g0_candidate,     .odds_ratio = 1,     .m         = n_candidate,     .n         = n_candidate,     .df        = df_candidate,     .alpha     = alpha,     .precision = 1e-3,     .superiority = FALSE   )      results <- rbind(results, data.frame(n = n_candidate, power = pwr * 100))      # Stop when desired power is reached:   if (pwr * 100 >= target_power) break    }   # Show last three rows: tail(results, 3) #>     n    power #> 33 42 77.75243 #> 34 43 79.13426 #> 35 44 80.10992

title: Overview of algorithm
text: modified test makes single change randomised (UMPU) Fisher test: instead always rejecting critical value (randomised) never rejecting (conservative), uses one global threshold \(\gamma_0\) rejects boundary boundary’s randomisation probability \(\gamma\) exceeds \(\gamma_0\). randomisation taken away, boundary rejection mass recovered. implementation chooses \(\gamma_0\) given null odds ratio, inverts resulting test p-value confidence interval. entry point modified_fisher_exact_test(): steps show function internally. Helper functions exported namespace prefixed ., can still accessed modifiedfisher::: (e.g. troubleshooting purposes, visualise steps example article).

text: Given frame candidate \(\gamma_0\), verdict table \((u, T)\) deterministic: \(u\) outside \([c_1, c_2]\): reject; \(u\) strictly inside \((c_1, c_2)\): accept; \(u = c_1\): reject iff \(\gamma_1 > \gamma_0\); \(u = c_2\): reject iff \(\gamma_2 > \gamma_0\); \(c_1 = c_2\) (single boundary point): reject iff \(\gamma_1 + \gamma_2 > \gamma_0\). .modified_reject(). Since verdict depends frame \(\gamma_0\), unknown success probability, whole rejection region built \((m+1)\times(n+1)\) 0/1 matrix (.build_rejection_matrix()), later steps avoid recomputing .

what: Step 3: Measure the (worst case) size over the nuisance parameter \(\pi_1\)
title: Overview of algorithm
text: \(\gamma_0\) acceptable test’s true (unconditional) size stays \(\alpha\). Conditional \(T\) test size-\(\alpha\) construction, unconditionally rejection probability depends unknown success rate \(\pi_1\). real size therefore function \(\pi_1\), need guard ‘worst case’. fixed \(\pi_1\), rejection probability sum tables weighted two binomial probabilities, computed bilinear form \(p_u^\top R\, p_v\) \(R\) rejection matrix Step 2 (local_size_modified()). size maximum \(\pi_1 \(0, 1)\) (size_modified()). Maximisation uses either "zoom" (grid, re-grid finer around peak, repeat; default, also SAS macro’s approach) "trust" (trust-region optimiser analytic gradient .local_size_gradient_modified()). size curve can multi-peaked, safest check plot via local_size_data = TRUE.
code: testframe <- construct_test_frame(.odds_ratio = 1, .m = 12, .n = 11, .alpha = 0.05, .precision = 1e-3)  # Worst-case size at a candidate gamma0 of 0.05: size_modified(.c = 0.05, .odds_ratio = 1, .m = 12, .n = 11, .df = testframe,               .alpha = 0.05, .precision = 1e-3, .method = "zoom",               .maze = 10, .zoom_iter = 6)

what: Step 4: Find the optimal \(\gamma_0\) by bisection
title: Overview of algorithm
text: still want largest worst-case size less \(\alpha\). size changes \(\gamma_0\) crosses one \(2(m+n+1)\) actual \(\gamma\) values Step 1, rather search continuum, sort values search among . Raising \(\gamma_0\) can remove boundaries, size monotone sorted index, makes bisection appropriate. done optimise_gamma0(): sorts pooled gamma1/gamma2 vector bisects largest threshold whose size exceed \(\alpha\). optimum single number half-open interval two adjacent sorted \(\gamma\)’s value gives test.

what: Step 5: Invert the test for the p-value and confidence interval
title: Overview of algorithm
text: Steps 1 4 decide, given null odds ratio \(\theta_0\), whether observed table rejected (.accept() helper function ties together: build frame, optimise \(\gamma_0\), apply rule). Confidence intervals p-values obtained inverting decision: Confidence interval: \(\theta_0\) rejected level \(\alpha\). edges found bisection, starting Woolf’s asymptotic limits moving inward first rejection, requested precision. P-value: smallest \(\alpha\) table rejected given null, found bisecting \(\alpha\). invert test, agree construction. main modified_fisher_exact_test() function runs following pipeline: construct_test_frame() → .modified_reject() / .build_rejection_matrix() → local_size_modified() / size_modified() → optimise_gamma0() → .accept() function returns htest $p.value,$estimate, $conf.int, optimal $gamma0, test frame, optionally size-versus-\(\pi_1\) dataframe:

Set precision smaller (e.g. 1e-4) returned confidence limits look imprecise, check returned p-value confidence interval still consistent. small tables. algorithm requires \(m + n \geq 2\). small tables (e.g. \(m = n = 2\)), test frame may rows optimised \(\gamma_0\) may 0 (borderline rejections), making modified test identical conservative test. Grid density size maximisation. dominant cost calls maximisation local size \(\pi_1\). controlled maze zoom_iter arguments (method = "zoom"). denser \(\pi_1\) grid (equivalent larger \(N\) paper’s notation) gives reliable size estimate takes proportionally longer: \(m = 65\), \(n = 71\) function takes roughly 1.5 seconds \(N = 20\) grid points around 20 seconds \(N = 300\). default settings (maze = 10, zoom_iter = 6) adequate purposes. title: Reproducing the paper's figures
text: Size function nuisance parameter \(\pi_1\). helper evaluates four tests grid \(\pi_1\) given sample sizes null odds ratio, returning data frame size percentages. needs optimal \(\gamma_0\) first. Note conservative test modified test \(\gamma_0 = 1\) (.e. boundary outcome ever rejected).
code: compute_size_curves <- function(m, n, alpha = 0.05, or = 1, precision = 1e-3,                                 p0_grid = seq(0.05, 0.95, by = 0.05),                                 method = "zoom", maze = 10, zoom_iter = 6) {    df <- construct_test_frame(.odds_ratio = or, .m = m, .n = n,                              .alpha = alpha, .precision = precision)    gamma0_opt <- optimise_gamma0(.odds_ratio = or, .m = m, .n = n,                                 .alpha = alpha, .precision = precision,                                 .method = method, .maze = maze,                                 .zoom_iter = zoom_iter)    data.frame(     p0 = p0_grid,     modified = sapply(p0_grid, function(p)       local_size_modified(p, .gamma0 = gamma0_opt, .odds_ratio = or,                           .m = m, .n = n, .df = df,                           .alpha = alpha, .precision = precision) * 100),     woolf = sapply(p0_grid, function(p)       local_size_asymptotic(p, .odds_ratio = or, .m = m, .n = n,                             .alpha = alpha) * 100),     sas_freq = sapply(p0_grid, function(p)       local_size_probability(p, .odds_ratio = or, .m = m, .n = n,                              .alpha = alpha) * 100),     conservative = sapply(p0_grid, function(p)       local_size_modified(p, .gamma0 = 1, .odds_ratio = or,                           .m = m, .n = n, .df = df,                           .alpha = alpha, .precision = precision) * 100)   ) }

title: Reproducing the paper's figures
text: Power function two response rates \(\pi_1\) \(\pi_2\). Holding \(\pi_1\) fixed, helper sweeps \(\pi_2\) returns test’s power percentage. modified conservative tests reuse test frame \(\gamma_0\) .
code: compute_power_curves <- function(m, n, pi1 = 0.2, alpha = 0.05, or = 1,                                  precision = 1e-3,                                  pi2_grid = seq(0.25, 0.6, by = 0.025),                                  method = "zoom", maze = 10, zoom_iter = 6) {    df <- construct_test_frame(.odds_ratio = or, .m = m, .n = n,                              .alpha = alpha, .precision = precision)    gamma0_opt <- optimise_gamma0(.odds_ratio = or, .m = m, .n = n,                                 .alpha = alpha, .precision = precision,                                 .method = method, .maze = maze,                                 .zoom_iter = zoom_iter)    data.frame(     pi2 = pi2_grid,     modified = sapply(pi2_grid, function(pi2)       power_modified(c(pi1, pi2), .gamma0 = gamma0_opt, .odds_ratio = or,                      .m = m, .n = n, .df = df, .alpha = alpha,                      .precision = precision, .superiority = FALSE) * 100),     woolf = sapply(pi2_grid, function(pi2)       power_asymptotic(c(pi1, pi2), .m = m, .n = n, .alpha = alpha) * 100),     sas_freq = sapply(pi2_grid, function(pi2)       power_probability(c(pi1, pi2), .m = m, .n = n, .alpha = alpha) * 100),     conservative = sapply(pi2_grid, function(pi2)       power_conservative(c(pi1, pi2), .m = m, .n = n, .df = df,                          .alpha = alpha, .precision = precision,                          .superiority = FALSE) * 100)   ) }

code: test_levels <- c("Modified", "SAS PROC Freq", "Woolf", "Conservative")  test_cols <- c(   "Modified"      = "#016c59",   "SAS PROC Freq" = "#3690c0",   "Woolf"         = "#67a9cf",   "Conservative"  = "grey70" )  test_lty <- c(   "Modified"      = 1,   "SAS PROC Freq" = 4,   "Woolf"         = 2,   "Conservative"  = 6 )  to_long <- function(df, value_col, key_col) {   df |>     pivot_longer(modified:conservative, names_to = "test",                  values_to = value_col) |>     mutate(test_label = factor(case_when(       test == "modified"     ~ "Modified",       test == "woolf"        ~ "Woolf",       test == "sas_freq"     ~ "SAS PROC Freq",       test == "conservative" ~ "Conservative"     ), levels = test_levels)) }

title: Reproducing the paper's figures
text: use \(m = 27\), \(n = 21\) null \(\mathrm{} = 1\), corresponds Figure 2() paper. modified test (solid) tracks just dashed α line across whole range, conservative test sits well .
code: alpha <- 0.05 size_df <- compute_size_curves(m = 27, n = 21, alpha = alpha, or = 1)  size_long <- to_long(size_df, "size", "test")  ggplot(size_long, aes(p0, size, colour = test_label, linetype = test_label)) +   geom_hline(yintercept = alpha * 100, linetype = 2, colour = "grey30") +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.9) +   scale_colour_manual(values = test_cols) +   scale_linetype_manual(values = test_lty) +   coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0, 1.1 * alpha * 100)) +   labs(x = expression(pi[1]), y = "Size (%)",        colour = NULL, linetype = NULL,        title = "Size of the modified Fisher exact test",        subtitle = "m = 27, n = 21, H0: OR = 1") +   theme_bw() +   theme(legend.position = "bottom")

what: Speed gain over the SAS macro
title: Reproducing the paper's figures
text: original paper reports CPU times roughly minute \(N = 50\) rising hour \(N = 300\), mainly size re-maximised nuisance parameter every grid point SAS macro. R function, using values, takes: roughly 1.5 seconds \(N = 20\) (.e. p0_grid = seq(0.05, 0.95, = 0.05)), roughly 3.6 seconds \(N = 50\), roughly 6.8 seconds \(N = 100\), roughly 20.2 seconds \(N = 300\). difference speed comes primarily rejection matrix constructed .build_rejection_matrix(). SAS macro, rejection rule re-evaluated every table \((u, T)\) grid point \(\pi_1\), mentioned . R implementation instead builds \((m+1) \times (n+1)\) rejection matrix \(R\) per \(\gamma_0\) candidate, evaluates local size \(\pi_1\) bilinear form \(p_u^\top R\, p_v\), \(p_u\) \(p_v\) binomial probability vectors Groups B. reduces size evaluation single matrix-vector multiplication, speeding process considerably.
code: # Figure 3(a) of the paper (slow): size_fig3a <- compute_size_curves(   m = 65, n = 71, or = 1,   p0_grid = seq(0.01, 0.99, by = 0.01) )  # Calculate time taken in R: # microbenchmark::microbenchmark(compute_size_curves(m = 65, n = 71, or = 1, p0_grid = seq(0.01, 0.99, by = 0.05)))

title: Reproducing the paper's figures
text: Two typos identified Table 2 van der Meulen, Raymond van der Meulen (2021) verifying package worked examples. modifiedfisher package produces correct numbers cases. Example 1 (5/12 vs 7/11): lower confidence limits missing leading zero. correct lower limit modified test 0.0716, printed 0.716 (likewise 0.076 0.760 Woolf, 0.055 0.550 Proc FREQ). misprint also self-contradictory: test-based interval (0.716, 2.210) exclude point estimate 0.408. Example 4: success count Group \(u = 71\), 72. heading “72/128 vs 58/142” incorrect. \(u = 71\), estimate (1.804), p-value (0.0175), confidence interval (1.108, 2.936) match printed values exactly; \(u = 72\) none . Examples 2 3 correct printed. typos confirmed using closed-form Woolf limits base R’s fisher.test(), neither relies package code.

what: Local size of Woolf's asymptotic test — local_size_asymptotic
title: Local size of Woolf's asymptotic test — local_size_asymptotic
text: Computes unconditional rejection probability (local size) Woolf's asymptotic Wald test \(H_0\): = \(\theta_0\) nuisance parameter \(p_0\), \(p_1 = p_0 / (p_0 + \theta_0 (1 - p_0))\). Rejects \(H_0\) \(|\log \hat{\theta} - \log \theta_0| / SE(\log \hat{\theta}) > z_{\alpha/2}\), \(\hat{\theta} = u(n-v) / ((m-u)v)\). Uses Haldane correction (replacing zero cells 0.5) handle tables one cells zero, matching SAS macro.

text: object class htest: list components p.value (two-sided test-based p-value, pvalue = TRUE), estimate (sample odds ratio), conf.int (two-sided \((1 - \alpha)\) confidence interval odds ratio, conf_int = TRUE), null.value (null odds ratio), alternative, method, data.name. additionally carries support.data (test frame construct_test_frame()), gamma0 (optimal threshold), power (power power_at_pi1 power_at_pi2, power = TRUE), local.size.data (data frame plotting size function nuisance parameter, local_size_data =   TRUE), fn_args (arguments used).

title: The non-conservative, size-\(\alpha\) modified Fisher exact test — modified_fisher_exact_test
code: # Example 1 of van der Meulen, Raymond & van der Meulen (2021): # 5/12 successes versus 7/11, testing H0: OR = 1 at alpha = 0.05. modified_fisher_exact_test(u = 5, m = 12, v = 7, n = 11, odds_ratio = 1) #>  #> 	Non-Conservative Size-α Modified Fisher's Exact Test #>  #> data:  u = 5, v = 7, m = 12, n = 11 #> p-value = 0.3208 #> alternative hypothesis: true odds ratio is not equal to 1 #> 95 percent confidence interval: #>  0.072034 2.209549 #> sample estimates: #> odds ratio  #>  0.4081633  #>   # \donttest{ # The size of the test as a function of the nuisance parameter can be # returned for diagnostic plotting (slower, as the size is evaluated on a # grid of 101 nuisance-parameter values): res <- modified_fisher_exact_test(u = 5, m = 12, v = 7, n = 11,                                   odds_ratio = 1, local_size_data = TRUE) head(res$local.size.data) #>    pi1         size method #> 1 0.00 0.0000000000   zoom #> 2 0.01 0.0006921852   zoom #> 3 0.02 0.0092790921   zoom #> 4 0.03 0.0393074943   zoom #> 5 0.04 0.1038314178   zoom #> 6 0.05 0.2116501340   zoom # }

title: Find the optimal \(\gamma_0\) — optimise_gamma0
text: Finds optimal \(\gamma_0\) threshold: largest value \(\gamma_0\) actual size MFET (maximum local size nuisance parameter \(p_0\)) exceed \(\alpha\). Uses bisection search \(2(m + n + 1)\) sorted randomisation probability values test frame.

title: Find the optimal \(\gamma_0\) — optimise_gamma0
text: .odds_ratio null hypothesis odds ratio \(\theta_0\). default. .m Number trials group 1. .n Number trials group 2. .alpha Nominal significance level \(\alpha\). default. .precision Numerical precision. default. .method Numerical method maximising local size: "zoom" "trust". .maze Number grid points evaluated zoom iteration. .zoom_iter Number zoom iterations ("zoom" method ).

title: Find the optimal \(\gamma_0\) — optimise_gamma0
text: single numeric value: optimal threshold \(\gamma_0\).

what: Power of Woolf's asymptotic test — power_asymptotic
title: Power of Woolf's asymptotic test — power_asymptotic
text: Computes unconditional power Woolf's asymptotic Wald test \(H_0\): = 1, response rates \((\pi_1, \pi_2)\). Rejects \(H_0\) \(|\log \hat{\theta}| / SE(\log \hat{\theta}) > z_{\alpha/2}\), \(\hat{\theta} = u(n-v) / ((m-u)v)\). Uses Haldane correction (replacing zero cells 0.5) tables one cells zero.

title: Power of Woolf's asymptotic test — power_asymptotic
Power of Woolf's asymptotic test — power_asymptotic
text: p Length-2 numeric vector \((\pi_1, \pi_2)\): success probability group 1 (\(\pi_1\)) group 2 (\(\pi_2\)) power evaluated. .m Number trials group 1. .n Number trials group 2. .alpha Nominal significance level \(\alpha\). default. .superiority Logical. TRUE, power computed tables observed rate group 2 exceeds group 1. Defaults FALSE.

title: Power of Woolf's asymptotic test — power_asymptotic
text: single numeric value: power Woolf's asymptotic test response rates \((\pi_1, \pi_2)\), \([0, 1]\).

title: Power of Woolf's asymptotic test — power_asymptotic

title: Power of the conservative Fisher exact test — power_conservative
text: Computes unconditional power conservative (non-randomised) Fisher exact test response rates \((\pi_1, \pi_2)\). Rejects \(H_0\) \(S\) falls strictly outside critical region \([c_1, c_2]\), equivalent setting \(\gamma_0 = 1\) MFET boundary values never rejected. test frame built relevant null odds ratio.

Power of the MFET — power_modified
text: Computes unconditional power MFET response rates \((\pi_1, \pi_2)\), .e. probability rejecting \(H_0\): = \(\theta_0\) true success probabilities \(\pi_1\) (group 1) \(\pi_2\) (group 2). test defined \(\gamma_0\) threshold test frame supplied. Optionally restricts superiority sub-region observed rate group 2 exceeds group 1.

title: Power of the MFET — power_modified
text: p Length-2 numeric vector \((\pi_1, \pi_2)\): success probability group 1 (\(\pi_1\)) group 2 (\(\pi_2\)) power evaluated. .gamma0 \(\gamma_0\) threshold: boundary values test frame rejected randomisation probability exceeds value. Typically output optimise_gamma0(). .odds_ratio Null hypothesis odds ratio \(\theta_0\) used build test frame. power evaluated response rates \((\pi_1,   \pi_2)\) supplied p, null. .m Number trials group 1. .n Number trials group 2. .df Test frame (data frame) generated construct_test_frame(), containing critical values \(c_1\), \(c_2\) randomisation probabilities \(\gamma_1\), \(\gamma_2\) every possible total \(T\). .alpha Nominal significance level \(\alpha\). default. .precision Numerical precision. default. .superiority Logical. TRUE, power computed tables observed rate group 2 exceeds group 1. Defaults FALSE.

text: single numeric value: power MFET response rates \((\pi_1, \pi_2)\), \([0, 1]\).

title: Power of the MFET — power_modified
code: df <- construct_test_frame(.odds_ratio = 1, .m = 6, .n = 4,                            .alpha = 0.05, .precision = 1e-3) g0 <- optimise_gamma0(.odds_ratio = 1, .m = 6, .n = 4, .alpha = 0.05,                       .precision = 1e-3, .method = "zoom", .maze = 10,                       .zoom_iter = 6) # Power against pi1 = 0.2, pi2 = 0.6: power_modified(p = c(0.2, 0.6), .gamma0 = g0, .odds_ratio = 1, .m = 6,            .n = 4, .df = df, .alpha = 0.05, .precision = 1e-3,            .superiority = FALSE) #> [1] 0.1755824

title: Power of the SAS Proc FREQ exact test — power_probability

what: P-value from the SAS Proc FREQ exact test — pvalue_probability
title: P-value from the SAS Proc FREQ exact test — pvalue_probability
text: Computes two-sided Fisher exact p-value \(H_0\): = odds_ratio summing probabilities conditional distribution \(U\) given \(T = t\) greater observed probability \(P_{\theta_0}(U = s \mid T = t)\). conditional distribution Fisher non-central hypergeometric specified odds ratio, computed via BiasedUrn::dFNCHypergeo(). odds_ratio = 1 reduces standard central hypergeometric, matching p-value reported SAS Proc FREQ \(H_0\): = 1.

title: P-value from the SAS Proc FREQ exact test — pvalue_probability
P-value from the SAS Proc FREQ exact test — pvalue_probability
text: s Number successes observed group 1. default. t Total number successes across groups (\(t = s + \) successes group 2); conditioning variable hypergeometric distribution. default. m Number trials group 1. default. n Number trials group 2. default. odds_ratio Null hypothesis odds ratio \(\theta_0\). Defaults 1.

title: P-value from the SAS Proc FREQ exact test — pvalue_probability
text: single numeric value: two-sided conditional exact p-value, \([0, 1]\).

title: P-value from the SAS Proc FREQ exact test — pvalue_probability
code: # Two-sided exact p-value for 5/12 vs 7/11 under H0: OR = 1 # (here t = u + v = 5 + 7 = 12): pvalue_probability(s = 5, t = 12, m = 12, n = 11, odds_ratio = 1) #> [1] 0.4136492

what: Actual size of the MFET for a given \(\gamma_0\) — size_modified
title: Actual size of the MFET for a given \(\gamma_0\) — size_modified
text: Finds actual size MFET given \(\gamma_0\) numerically maximising local size nuisance parameter \(p_0 \(0, 1)\). Supports two methods: "zoom" evaluates local size grid points iteratively refines maximum (matches SAS macro default); "trust" uses trust-region optimiser analytic gradient .local_size_gradient_modified().

title: Actual size of the MFET for a given \(\gamma_0\) — size_modified
code: df <- construct_test_frame(.odds_ratio = 1, .m = 6, .n = 4,                            .alpha = 0.05, .precision = 1e-3) # Actual size of the test at a candidate gamma0 threshold of 0.05: size_modified(.c = 0.05, .odds_ratio = 1, .m = 6, .n = 4, .df = df,           .alpha = 0.05, .precision = 1e-3, .method = "zoom",           .maze = 10, .zoom_iter = 6) #> [1] 0.2170814

what: Consistent naming scheme for the five test variants
title: modifiedfisher 0.0.3
text: comparator test functions renamed five variants follow single descriptive axis, instead earlier mix acronym (mfet), software product (procfreq), author (woolf). variant now named construction: modified - non-conservative, size-α modified Fisher exact test (package’s test). randomised - fully randomised UMPU Fisher exact test. conservative - conservative, non-randomised Fisher exact test. probability - probability-method two-sided exact p-value (p-value reported SAS Proc FREQ). asymptotic - Woolf’s asymptotic (Wald) test. size_*, local_size_*, power_* families use suffixes, file names updated match. exported internal renames : local_size_randomised(), power_randomised(), power_conservative() already -scheme unchanged. breaking change: code written v0.0.2 comparator function names need updating new names.
